words: This conversation with HARRY SAMPSON and CLARICE MOODY SAMPSON took place at
their home in Moscow on August 16, 1976. The interviewer was SAM SCHRAGER.
CLARICE SAMPSON: How we got hold of thatvmy sister had a friend in California
and she wrote and course, she sent it up here so we could read it. I'm
going to get out now so Harry will talk to you.
SAM SCHRAGER: I don't want you to leave Clarice. I'm going to ask you some too.
But what was the matter with blood on the table and the pig?
HARRY SAMPSON: Well, it's just the idea that there was blood on the table; the
pig that he saw in a vision, wasn't it? Of some kind? I never read
it, so I don't know about it.

speaker: CS
words: We aren't taping now, are we?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I just turned it on. Don't stop talking.

speaker: CS
words: I'm not sure though about this- you shouldn't say things-

speaker: HS
words: Is that on now?

speaker: SS
words: I just turned it on.

speaker: HS
words: Well, I first met Dr. Robinson when he came to Moscow, came into the

speaker: HS
words: store, David's store, and Mr. David introduced me to him because he'd

speaker: HS
words: interested in Boy Scout work, too and he thought perhaps I could

speaker: HS
words: use him in my Scout work. And so that way, I got acquainted with him.

speaker: HS
words: Well, Mr. Robinson evidently came up here without any fin ances to

speaker: HS
words: speak of. He got this job at the drugstore across the street, Charlie

speaker: HS
words: drugstore. But Mr. David, in order for him to have a place to

speaker: HS
words: sleep, he furnished him a bed and blankets and whatnot, in order to

speaker: HS
words: have a place to sleep. Well, it wasn't long after that he got,- I

speaker: HS
words: think there were six fellow,- I know that George Benson was one, Oscar

speaker: HS
words: Anderson was one, and Anderson of the First National Bank-

speaker: SS
words: Elmer Anderson.

speaker: HS
words: Elmer Anderson, yes. And I don't know just how much they financed him

speaker: HS
words: to, but it must have been around $1,000 a piece, and it was through

speaker: HS
words: that capital that he got his Psychiana going. Well, it just took like

speaker: HS
words: wildfire all over the world because he advertised in the Pathfinder

speaker: HS
words: magazine in Washington, D. C. and that had a world circulation. And

speaker: HS
words: orders came in. Well, I think he operated about three years and he

speaker: HS
words: bought these fellows out, and then is when the money started coming

speaker: HS
words: in. And he started giving lectures. He'd go to Los Angeles, or he'd

speaker: HS
words: go back to New York and give a lecture and through these lectures he

speaker: HS
words: got more- sold more courses. And in these courses; living in Moscow

speaker: HS
words: he would not sell them to anyone in Moscow, they had to be ou t of the

speaker: HS
words: city. Well it wasn't long until he bought a home here with the money

speaker: HS
words: he had. Put in a pipe organ. He was a musician too, as well, an or

speaker: HS
words: ganist. And then along the way he got into trouble on his citizenship.

speaker: HS
words: He was a son of a Baptist minister up in Canada and he came over into

speaker: HS
words: this country and I don't know who dug it up on him, but he was not an

speaker: HS
words: American citizen and so, he had to leave the country. And, I've for

speaker: HS
words: gotten, he went to one of the islands, either Puerto Rico or Cuba or

speaker: HS
words: someplace in the islands there and Senator Borah helped him straighten

speaker: HS
words: the thing out for him, and he came back into the country and things

speaker: HS
words: were alright from then on. Well, the Moscow Medical Building, right

speaker: HS
words: back of the First National Bank, he built that and he had his office

speaker: HS
words: in there. Well, I was well enough acquainted with him- he only lived

speaker: HS
words: a block from us- and he invited me down one day to his office, just to

speaker: HS
words: see what it was like. So I went in there and he was telling me all

speaker: HS
words: about this citizenship setup that Borah took care of him on. Well,

speaker: HS
words: he says, "I'll tell you another thing," he says, "I've got a tape re

speaker: HS
words: corder underneath this desk," tje says, "anybody that comes in

speaker: HS
words: this office that I'm not sure of," he says, "I turn that on, 'cause I'm

speaker: HS
words: not going to have them have anything on me."

speaker: HS
words: He was very clever about

speaker: HS
words: that end of it, he wasn't going to be caught into another lawsuit or

speaker: HS
words: something. Well, it wasn't long after that that he- oh, he just kind

speaker: HS
words: of threw money away there for a while in Moscow. He established Robin

speaker: HS
words: son's Lake out here. There was a group of men in town here thought

speaker: HS
words: that might be a pretty good resort place out there and they'd build

speaker: HS
words: summer homes out there; well, he got a hold of that, and he just beat

speaker: HS
words: 'em to that and he bought that and turned it over to the county as a

speaker: HS
words: recreatiorc'area. Well, that was one thing. Well, then the newspapers

speaker: HS
words: were kind of tying into him a little here; newspaper, rather. So he

speaker: HS
words: decided that he was just going to run that paper himself so he bought

speaker: HS
words: 'em out. And he owned the Star Mirror until he turned it over to the -

speaker: HS
words: got Bill Marineau from Elk Rfver to come over here and edit the paper,

speaker: HS
words: and they changed the name to the Idahonian. Well, then there was another

speaker: HS
words: setup; he was a pharmacist, and he felt the pharmacists were more or

speaker: HS
words: less gouging the people with their prescriptions and their drugs and so

speaker: HS
words: on. So, he opened up a or he bought a pharmacy- opened up. Walgren's

speaker: HS
words: is now, and he operated that until whatyoucallems took it over. So he

speaker: HS
words: was a promoter, from 'way back.

speaker: CS
words: Yes, and tell about the Italian setup.

speaker: HS
words: When I was visiting.in his office he was telling me about an Egyptian

speaker: HS
words: Shah or prince that had a son that was ill and he'd been taking his

speaker: HS
words: course- he had six or eight courses- and.after you took them you started another list-

speaker: HS
words: you get em going on another; kept the finances

speaker: HS
words: coming in. Well, anyway, this prince cabled him and told him about his

speaker: HS
words: son. He says, "Maybe you can do something for him." And I suppose he

speaker: HS
words: wrote him back that he had prayed for him and done this and that, and

speaker: HS
words: the son became well. It was a kind of a miracle;, the prince. So the

speaker: HS
words: prince sent him a check, and if I'm not mistaken it was $5,000 or

speaker: HS
words: $10,000, and Robinson told me that he sent that check right back to him

speaker: HS
words: and told him that the publicity that he would get on that would far out

speaker: HS
words: rank what he'd paid him. So that's the unusualness about him in all

speaker: HS
words: his affairs.

speaker: SS
words: Did he seem sincere to you, Harry?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, yes. Yes, I think.he was sincere in his work because he and Drury

speaker: HS
words: used to have conflabs, they were good friends, too, and Drury told him

speaker: HS
words: that he was getting all his information from Mary Baker Eddy. He revolved his science

speaker: HS
words: around Mary Baker Eddy. And he ;. he had a lot

speaker: HS
words: of that. He was a smart promoter.

speaker: SS
words: What about the struggle with George Lamphere? According to Robinson's

speaker: SS
words: book, Strange Autobiography, that book of his, he lays his troubles

speaker: SS
words: with the citizenship and all that on Lamphere, because George Lamphere

speaker: SS
words: hated him so much that he was after him.

speaker: HS
words: Oh, yes. Well, he owned the Star Mirror; the Moscow paper, and he

speaker: HS
words: was tying into him and there was nothing he could say but Vfhat was de

speaker: HS
words: trimental to Robinson. And then Robinson began thinking he'd take

speaker: HS
words: over the paper.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I believe, probably- you see, he had so much printing to do with

speaker: CS
words: all this- and I think probably they just didn't agree about the prin

speaker: CS
words: ting or something. So that was when Dr. Robinson got Bill Marineau to

speaker: CS
words: come from Elk River, he was running a little paper up there, and to

speaker: CS
words: start up a paper. And then, of course, for a while both papers were

speaker: CS
words: just at each other all the time, you know. But it seemed strange and

speaker: CS
words: funny to us, because the Star Mirror finally combined with the other

speaker: CS
words: paper. But Lamphere was out of that because Boaz was the editor

speaker: CS
words: for Lamphere and then he went in-

speaker: HS
words: When Lamphere sold it he had to take Louis Boaz over as the editor,

speaker: HS
words: and he was with the Idahonian up until four or five years ago.

speaker: CS
words: They did all the printing then for Psychiana.

speaker: SS
words: What I wonder about, is why Lamphere disliked him so much as Robinson

speaker: SS
words: indicated in the book, was largely because of the printing.

speaker: CS
words: I think so.

speaker: SS
words: And he said that Lamphere was charging twice what Bill Marineau said

speaker: SS
words: it would cost, so that he thought he was getting gouged by Lamphere.

speaker: HS
words: Well, he took over the Queen City Printing Company, that's where he did

speaker: HS
words: his printing and that's where Bill Marineau took charge there. Then

speaker: HS
words: that got to be too small and he opened up a building up where the Weizel

speaker: HS
words: Insurance Company is now, and he must have employed, ten, twelve people

speaker: HS
words: up there.

speaker: CS
words: He changed the rating of our postoffice here in Moscow, the amount

speaker: CS
words: of the mail he sent out.

speaker: SS
words: To what extent do you think there was religious opposition to him, be

speaker: SS
words: cause of his seemingly antidenominational views?

speaker: ES
words: Indirectly I think they were against him, but speaking from the pulpit

speaker: ES
words: they weren't.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I think some churches were, Harry, because they thought it was

speaker: CS
words: just an ism of some kind. But his wife and his son and daughter came

speaker: CS
words: to the Presbyterian Church; they were all members, but still they were

speaker: CS
words: in on Psychiana, too. So he had that tie, because when his daughter

speaker: CS
words: would appear in Sunday School pageants and so on, why Dr. Robinson al

speaker: CS
words: ways came. But there was one thing about Dr. Robinson, he did an awful lot for poor

speaker: CS
words: people. And at Christmastime

speaker: CS
words: number of sacks of flour and turkeys and the things that he distributed

speaker: CS
words: over Latah County was amazing. And he never boasted about it. It was

speaker: CS
words: never told how much he was doing.

speaker: HS
words: Well, you know, when the Presbyterian Church he pledged money to build

speaker: HS
words: the tower of the church; Presbyterian Church. I think he did it because

speaker: HS
words: his folks, or his wife, was a member of the church. He didn't give it,

speaker: HS
words: no.

speaker: HS
words: Not that much, no.

speaker: HS
words: That was the thing that was brought up- then he was down in San Fran

speaker: HS
words: cisco; I heard this indirectly, of course, and it came to him that the

speaker: HS
words: church were not just satisfied to have him do that, or pay for that

speaker: HS
words: tower, and he withdrew his pledge. I heard that, yes. Yes, that was

speaker: HS
words: a fact.

speaker: HS
words: This charity that he gave to people- poor people- was it more than just

speaker: HS
words: at Christmastime?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, yes, I think he was charitable the whole year. I think he did for

speaker: HS
words: people, but I think Christmas was a big time that he just wanted t o

speaker: HS
words: help people.

speaker: HS
words: Would these names, where people would go, do you think directly to him?

speaker: HS
words: I don't know hcNM he handled it. That was before the days of Christmas

speaker: HS
words: baskets and so on. He just had his own Christmas donations that he

speaker: HS
words: made.

speaker: HS
words: Then he didn't try to capitalize on it?

speaker: HS
words: No, not to my knowledge at all. Because a lot of people don't even

speaker: HS
words: know that Dr. Robinson did it. I want to tell you one thing though, we

speaker: HS
words: had been in Dearborn, Michigan and out to Greenfield Village and our

speaker: HS
words: car was rifled there, and we drove away from Greenfield Village feeling

speaker: HS
words: that the police weren't doing much about it. And we landed that night

speaker: HS
words: in Green Rivers- where was it?

speaker: HS
words: That was in northern Michigan some place.

speaker: HS
words: Northern Michigan, a little town, and pouring rain and we didn't know

speaker: HS
words: where we were going to stay. Well, we saw this guest house, so we went

speaker: HS
words: to the door and told her there were three of us; our son was with us,

speaker: HS
words: that we wanted a place to stay overnight. So she .had us come in. It

speaker: HS
words: was a nice home; spiral stairway and a very pretty home. And the minute

speaker: HS
words: Harry registered in her guest book, she saw Moscow, Idaho and she said,

speaker: HS
words: "Do you happen to know Dr. Robinson? Dr. Frank Robinson?" And Harry

speaker: HS
words: told her yes, he was a neighbor of ours. Well, right away we were the

speaker: HS
words: most welcome guests you ever saw because she was such a firm follower

speaker: HS
words: of him.

speaker: HS
words: She was taking the course. (Chuckles)

speaker: CS
words: But when she saw that Moscow, Idaho, she just- we told her we saw him

speaker: CS
words: every day go past our house and that his daughter and our son went to

speaker: CS
words: the same birthday parties and so on, and oh, she just was so interested.

speaker: HS
words: Wasn't there another incident where he helped? There were some young

speaker: HS
words: sters lost out here around Robinson's Lake and he hired a helicopter to

speaker: HS
words: come in here and hunt those kids.

speaker: CS
words: Or a plane, or something.

speaker: SS
words: What about the fact that a lot of people seemed to think that he wasn't

speaker: SS
words: sincere? There were people that seemed to have the idea that he was in

speaker: SS
words: it just for money. I look at those books of hi$, I think, that this man

speaker: SS
words: must have believed what he was saying, because it seems to heartfelt to

speaker: SS
words: me, what he's saying. But there are people that just seem to feel that

speaker: SS
words: he couldn't have been sincere.

speaker: HS
words: When you read the book, the first one explains what Psychiana

speaker: HS
words: was, you would say he was sincere about it.

speaker: CS
words: Well, what makes me dubious about his sincerity was that- I don't know

speaker: CS
words: what position Mrs. Robinson had in his setup-

speaker: HS
words: Bishop.

speaker: CS
words: A bishop. And his son had a certain thing and a title and in order to

speaker: CS
words: do that they could escape paying a lot of tax.

speaker: HS
words: Yes, so does a religious organization. Of course, when he passed away,

speaker: HS
words: that's when she was made a bishop, and then the son was supposed to

speaker: HS
words: take over. He was a graduate of Stanford University, he had a good

speaker: HS
words: education. And the son run it for oh, maybe a year or two and he just

speaker: HS
words: couldn't go along with it, I guess.

speaker: CS
words: Couldn't swallow it.

speaker: HS
words: And he wouldn't sell it. He could have sold it for thousands of dol

speaker: HS
words: lars. There was a concern in St. Louis wanted to take it over and he

speaker: HS
words: wouldn't sell it, and he just simply closed it up and no one has ever

speaker: HS
words: had access to it. It died with Robinson.

speaker: CS
words: All of it is at the University of Idaho; all of that

speaker: SS
words: I think it will be, I think there's about a twenty-five year limit on

speaker: SS
words: it.

speaker: HS
words: If you want contacts, he has a - the girl out here? Is she a daughter?

speaker: HS
words: No, that's Alfred's daughter.A You could probably get some information

speaker: HS
words: from her, too. She lives right out here someplace on an acreage.

speaker: SS
words: Well, Alfred, do you think he had been a follower before that, or do

speaker: SS
words: you think he just tried to—?

speaker: CS
words: I think he tried to. I hate to say these things.

speaker: SS
words: Well, it's the historical truth. I think it's important.

speaker: CS
words: Alfred, he just became almost an alcoholic because he didn't want to

speaker: CS
words: go along with that and he felt he owed it to his father or something.

speaker: CS
words: So then he- after he would have nothing more to do with the Idahonian

speaker: CS
words: why, he sold advertising for the Idahonian. They owned the stock still,

speaker: CS
words: he and his mother. And finally though, when Mrs. Robinson moved to

speaker: CS
words: California, we know that she sold her stock to the firm as is now be

speaker: CS
words: cause Mr.  told us so.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know what made them decide they didn't want to stay any longer?

speaker: CS
words: Well, her health- she needed to be in a warmer climate and Alfred was

speaker: CS
words: still here at that time when Mrs. Robinson moved to California but the

speaker: CS
words: daughter lived away from here. And now, Mrs. Robinson is living in

speaker: CS
words: Texas with her daughter. And I think Florence's husband has retired.

speaker: CS
words: He was a career man in the army, but they like it in Texas and^Mrs.

speaker: CS
words: Robinson is crippled from arthritis. She remarried in California, Mrs

speaker: CS
words: Robinson.and we visited them in their apartment; went to call on themat-

speaker: CS
words: where was it? Near Monteray, there somepM.ce. Carmel. And

speaker: CS
words: we went to call on them and she seemed much better to us then , but after

speaker: CS
words: her second husband died her health went downhill

speaker: SS
words: Did you know her very well?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes. She was a most charming woman, a most charming wcman. And

speaker: CS
words: she was delightful to visit with and had a good mind and she was a great

speaker: CS
words: worker in the Eastern Star. But she was just a most charming person.

speaker: CS
words: And she was a great Daughter of the American Revolution member.

speaker: HS
words: Came from Klamath Falls, Oregon.

speaker: CS
words: Her father was a judge or something at Klamath Falls.

speaker: SS
words: That's what I read. She, according to him, she was really a steadfast

speaker: SS
words: helper to him, that she really helped him a great deal, is what he said.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I am sure she never grumbled of anything, she just wasn't that

speaker: CS
words: kind of a person. She was a most pleasing person to be with.

speaker: HS
words: He was a family man. He sure did everything in the world for his wife

speaker: HS
words: and son and daughter.

speaker: CS
words: Harry used to measure him for suits; custom made suits. And he got

speaker: CS
words: one one time, a blue suit with a white stripe through it, you know, and

speaker: CS
words: he liked it, so ordered another one just like it. He was

speaker: CS
words: very particular. He wore a wide brimmed Stetson or something, always.

speaker: HS
words: Yes, he sent me a picture after I sold him that suit with his daughter

speaker: HS
words: on his lap. It was a picture about this big, and he gave me one of 'em.

speaker: HS
words: You gave it back to the daughter.

speaker: CS
words: I gave it to the daughter.

speaker: HS
words: And he liked that suit so well that he ordered a second one just like

speaker: HS
words: it. Here's another thing- speaking about his going- he told me, he

speaker: HS
words: says, any time that he wanted to raise three or four thousand dollars

speaker: HS
words: all he had to do is give a lecture someplace, and Los Angeles was an

speaker: HS
words: awful good place to do that. Well, he gave lectures in a lot of places.

speaker: HS
words: But the first suit that I sold him, quite early in his Psychiana v

speaker: HS
words: he was giving a lecture back in Rochester, New York, and it was when

speaker: HS
words: the zippers first came out in men's suits; in the trousers and they

speaker: HS
words: didn't lock at the top and you just roll it up there- and he reared

speaker: HS
words: out, like this, he did that when he was speaking, he'd rear out.

speaker: HS
words: that, and that zipper started coming down and a fellow had to call his

speaker: HS
words: attention to it! (Chuckles)

speaker: CS
words: Well, I thought he got his shirt caught in it then.

speaker: HS
words: No, that was somebody else. (Chuckles)

speaker: CS
words: He was a big man.

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, he was about six foot one tall, I guess and a hundred and ninetyfive

speaker: HS
words: pounds, somewhere around there.

speaker: CS
words: And handsome. Very handsome. We used to see him walk to his office

speaker: CS
words: every day past our house.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that he was very outgoing- very forthright, forceful. Is that

speaker: SS
words: true? In the way he spoke?

speaker: CS
words: I don't know, I never heard him speak.

speaker: SS
words: Well, just in conversation.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I didn't ever have conversation, Harry did. Was he very outgoing

speaker: CS
words: in his conversation, Harry?

speaker: HS
words: Oh, yes. Yes, I would say so.

speaker: CS
words: I never did visit with him, only speaking, of course.

speaker: HS
words: We were pretty good friends. I know one day I was working out in the

speaker: HS
words: yard, we lived on the corner of Third and 'rblt^. And a fellow came

speaker: HS
words: by with a bouquet in his hand, of roses, and he says, "Can you tell me

speaker: HS
words: where Doc Robinson lives?" I says, "Yes, just around the corner here."

speaker: HS
words: "Well," he says, "I want to present him with this bouquet." He had

speaker: HS
words: read a lot about Doc Robinson and he wanted to meet him. What he had

speaker: HS
words: in his Psychiana courses took with a lot of people; it must have been

speaker: HS
words: convincing to a lot of people.

speaker: SS
words: I have the impression that it was and I'm interested- if I had the

speaker: SS
words: chance to study some of the materials that they have, the letters that

speaker: SS
words: they got, I'd be interested to try to get a feeling for what kind of

speaker: SS
words: people subscribed to his teaching. If there were certain types of peo

speaker: SS
words: ple, because they were from all over, weren't they?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah. Well, you know, there's always people looking for- that are not

speaker: HS
words: quite satisfied with the religion,u church that they re connected

speaker: HS
words: with, and he had something in those lectures that appealed to 'em and

speaker: HS
words: those were the people that went along with him. Now Vincent Peale, of

speaker: HS
words: New York City got his material; he told me that, too. He may have

speaker: HS
words: taken out of curiosity to see if there was anything in it that he could

speaker: HS
words: use in his preaching.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I think that was Dr. Drury's forte with Dr. Robinson. He was

speaker: CS
words: just interested to know what he felt and how he was doing his teaching.

speaker: CS
words: I don't think he was for him or against him, I think it was just an in

speaker: CS
words: terest in knowing-

speaker: HS
words: Dr. Drury was this type of a preacher, he was always wanting to delve

speaker: HS
words: into things. And there was one winter that he had preachers of every

speaker: HS
words: church in town on a Thursday night come out; we had a Thursday night

speaker: HS
words: group, and explain their particular church history and their philoso

speaker: HS
words: phy and so on. And he even got a Catholic priest there.

speaker: SS
words: Was this at the Presbyterian church, that this took place?

speaker: HS
words: At the Presbyterian Church.

speaker: SS
words: And anyone could come and hear this?

speaker: HS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go to these meetings?

speaker: HS
words: Yep. And that's why he accused Psychiana of copying Mary Baker Eddy.

speaker: HS
words: That's where he based his whole Psychiana on-

speaker: CS
words: Dr. Drury was all for research and he'd researched everything.

speaker: SS
words: Robinson claimed in Strange Autobiography that Drury had helped dis

speaker: SS
words: suade some of the ministers in Moscow from ganging up on him, because

speaker: SS
words: Drury had said, "if there is something behind this movement it'll suc

speaker: SS
words: ceed no matter what you do, and if it's not, it'll fail by itself."

speaker: SS
words: He said Drury stuck up for him that way.

speaker: CS
words: Well, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Does that seem likely?

speaker: CS
words: Very likely.

speaker: HS
words: Well, you know, I always feel like- we had a Mrs. Sargent that was a

speaker: HS
words: German teacher at the college, and she filled the pulpit at the Pres

speaker: HS
words: byterian Church a couple of Sundays, and I'll never forget she made

speaker: HS
words: this statement, "There's good in all forms of religion regardless of

speaker: HS
words: what is was. Well, there s Mohamadism or Judism or what it is, as long

speaker: HS
words: as they have a moral standard." I've always remembered that because,

speaker: HS
words: you go to criticizing a lot of- like Jehovah Witnesses and all thosethey

speaker: HS
words: have a certain moral standard.

speaker: CS
